title: Layered glaze dip: two-colour effect
subtitle: Dipping a bisque piece in two glazes at different heights to create a colour break and overlap zone
excerpt: Two-colour layered dipping applies a base glaze all over, then partially dips in a second contrasting glaze that overlaps the first to a set depth. In the overlap zone where both glazes sit, the glaze layers melt and interact, producing a third colour or a mottled blend. Test tiles are required before applying to a piece.
sourceNotes: Layered glaze dipping technique derived from first principles.

attrs.body: Two-layer glazing can produce thicker glaze coats that drip in the kiln if the piece is not set up on a catch tray or waster shelf. Ensure each layer is within the thickness the manufacturer specifies. Glaze contains metal oxides; wash hands after handling liquid glaze. Ventilate the kiln area during glaze firing.
attrs.title: Glaze and kiln safety

content.text: Two-colour
glaze layering

is one of the most direct ways to get complex surface effects in ceramics without specialised equipment. A base glaze coat covers the whole piece; a second glaze is applied over the lower half or two-thirds, leaving a horizontal colour break. In the
melt-through

zone where both glazes sit, unexpected interactions often occur; the two colours merge, one bleeds into the other, or a new colour appears. Because glaze interactions are not fully predictable, test tiles before applying to a finished piece.

items.name: Base glaze (colour 1)
items.quantity: enough to dip the piece fully

content.text: Test tiles first

Before applying two glazes to a finished piece, fire at least four test tiles: one with base glaze only, one with second glaze only, and two with both glazes at different overlap depths. The overlap zone behaviour can only be known from firing. Glaze combinations that look complementary as liquids may fire to very different colours.
Choosing compatible glazes

Both glazes must fire to the same cone temperature. Mixing low-fire and mid-fire glazes produces underfired or overfired zones. Glazes from the same manufacturer are most reliably compatible. Good starting combinations are: a satin white base with a celadon second coat; a clear glaze base with a blue second coat; a warm amber base with a black tenmoku-style second coat.
The two-dip method
1. Wax and first dip
Wax the foot ring. Stir the base glaze thoroughly and dip the whole
bisqueware
piece in the base glaze, covering the full exterior. Allow to dry fully; at least 20-30 minutes, until the glaze coat is no longer cool to the touch.
2. Mark the overlap line
Decide where the second glaze will reach to. A line one-third to halfway up the piece typically produces a good balance. Mark this line lightly on the dried first glaze with a pencil.
3. Partial dip in the second glaze

Hold the piece by the rim and lower it into the second glaze bucket up to the marked line. Submerge for two seconds and lift out with a slight rotation. The second glaze will sit on top of the dried first glaze. If the combined thickness feels excessive (if the glaze layer is noticeably raised) reduce the dip time or thin the second glaze slightly.
Loading and firing

Place the piece on a kiln shelf with a waster tile beneath or on a raised setter, in case the double-glaze coat runs more than a single coat would. Leave at least 20 mm clearance from adjacent kiln shelves. Fire to the manufacturer's specified cone. Do not adjust the firing schedule for two-coat work; the dwell at peak temperature is the same.
What it looks like when it is right

The upper section shows the base glaze colour. The lower section shows the second glaze colour. In the overlap zone (typically 15-25 mm wide) the two glazes have merged into a broken, mottled, or blended third colour. The transition line is not sharp but diffuse. The overall surface is smooth and glassy from the glaze melt.

items.fix: Test tiles first; place a waster tile beneath the piece; consider reducing one dip time to thin the combined coat
items.cause: Combined glaze too thick, or glaze too fluid for the cone temperature
items.symptom: Glaze runs heavily off the base in firing

items.fix: Choose more contrasting glaze colours; limit the second dip depth to one-third of the piece height
items.cause: Two glazes too similar in colour, or the second glaze completely dominates
items.symptom: No visible colour transition. The piece looks like one flat colour

items.fix: Allow the first coat to dry completely; allow at least 30 minutes, then dip the second coat quickly to minimise total thickness
items.cause: First glaze coat not fully dry before second dip, or combined coat too thick
